LibreTexts GOB (Roosevelt) — Ch. 16.3 Ch. 3 Odd Answers (answer key)

meta.notes: General-chemistry MCQ scraped verbatim from the chapter 'Multiple-Choice Questions' sections. The GOB book's Chapter 16 answer key lists answers for ALL ten MCQ in each chapter (not just odds), so every item here is keyed to source-answer-key and was additionally cross-checked against the source text. Mirrors the cpbs-001..004 items already in questions.json (same book, later chapters).

questions.stem: Which of the following has a positive charge?
questions.explanation: A proton carries a +1 elementary charge. Electrons are negatively charged, neutrons are neutral, and a neutral hydrogen atom has no net charge.

hints.prompt: Of an electron and a neutron, what are their electric charges?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The electron is negative and the neutron is neutral

choices.label: C
choices.text: The electron is positive and the neutron is negative

hints.rationale: Electrons carry a negative charge and neutrons are electrically neutral.

hints.prompt: Why is a complete neutral atom, such as a hydrogen atom, not a charged particle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its equal numbers of protons and electrons make the charges cancel

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has far more electrons than protons

hints.rationale: A neutral atom has equal positive and negative charges that sum to zero.

hints.prompt: Which listed choice is the main trap, appearing positive only because it contains a proton?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hydrogen atom, which is neutral overall

choices.label: B
choices.text: An electron, which is actually neutral

choices.label: C
choices.text: A neutron, which is actually positive

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no trap; every choice is positive

hints.rationale: A neutral hydrogen atom contains a proton but also an electron, so it has no net charge.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q1 = a).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the mass number for the isotope Br-79 (79Br)?
questions.explanation: In the notation 79Br the leading number is the mass number (protons + neutrons) = 79. 35 is bromine's atomic number, 44 is the neutron count (79 - 35), and 79.904 is the element's average atomic mass.

hints.prompt: In isotope notation such as (mass number)X, what does the leading superscript number represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mass number, equal to protons plus neutrons

hints.rationale: The leading superscript is the mass number, the sum of protons and neutrons.

hints.prompt: For 79Br, bromine's atomic number is 35. If you instead wanted the NEUTRON count, what would you compute?
hints.rationale: Neutrons = mass number - atomic number = 79 - 35 = 44, which is the neutron count, not the mass number.

hints.prompt: Which value is included only to tempt you into reporting the periodic-table average mass instead of a single isotope's mass number?
hints.rationale: 79.904 amu is bromine's weighted average over all isotopes, not the mass number of one isotope.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q2 = c).

questions.stem: How many electrons are present in Mg2+?
questions.explanation: A neutral magnesium atom has 12 electrons; the 2+ cation has lost 2 electrons, leaving 10.

hints.prompt: How does forming a cation with charge n+ change an atom's electron count?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It removes n electrons from the neutral atom

choices.label: B
choices.text: It adds n electrons to the neutral atom

choices.label: C
choices.text: It removes n protons from the nucleus

choices.label: D
choices.text: It leaves the electron count unchanged

hints.rationale: A positive charge of n+ means n electrons were lost.

hints.prompt: A neutral magnesium atom has 12 electrons. How do you get the electron count of Mg2+?
hints.rationale: The 2+ charge means two electrons were removed from the 12 of neutral Mg.

hints.prompt: Which choice is the trap for students who forget the ion lost electrons and just report neutral magnesium's count?
hints.rationale: 12 is the electron count of a neutral Mg atom, before the 2+ charge removes any electrons.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q3 = a).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct set of protons, neutrons, and electrons for Cd-113 as the 2+ cation (113Cd2+)?
questions.explanation: Cadmium has atomic number 48 (48 protons). Mass number 113 means 113 - 48 = 65 neutrons. A 2+ charge means two fewer electrons than protons: 46 electrons.

hints.prompt: For an ion written as (mass)X with a given charge, how are protons, neutrons, and electrons found?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protons = atomic number Z; neutrons = mass - Z; electrons = Z minus the charge

choices.label: B
choices.text: Protons = mass number; neutrons = Z; electrons = mass number

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protons = mass - Z; neutrons = Z; electrons = Z

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protons = Z; neutrons = mass number; electrons = Z

hints.rationale: Z gives protons, mass minus Z gives neutrons, and the ion charge adjusts the electron count.

hints.prompt: Cadmium has Z = 48, and this ion has mass number 113 and a 2+ charge. Which setup is correct?
hints.rationale: Neutrons = 113 - 48, and because the ion is 2+, electrons = 48 - 2.

hints.prompt: The 2+ ion should have fewer than 48 electrons. Which listed electron count is a trap because it corresponds to GAINING electrons?
hints.rationale: 50 electrons would mean two were added (an anion); a 2+ cation must have fewer than the neutral atom's 48.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q4 = c).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct chemical symbol for copper?
questions.explanation: Copper's symbol is Cu (from the Latin cuprum). C is carbon, Co is cobalt, and Cr is chromium.

hints.prompt: Where do one- and two-letter chemical symbols like 'Cu' come from?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Standardized abbreviations, several derived from an element's Latin name

choices.label: B
choices.text: Always the first two letters of the English name

choices.label: C
choices.text: The element's atomic number written in Roman numerals

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are chosen arbitrarily by each chemist

hints.rationale: Symbols are standardized, and several (like Cu from cuprum) come from Latin names.

hints.prompt: Three of the choices are symbols for other elements. Which element does the symbol 'Co' represent?
hints.rationale: Co is cobalt, a different element from copper.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are the symbols for carbon and chromium, not copper?
hints.rationale: C is carbon and Cr is chromium, so both can be ruled out.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q5 = d).

questions.stem: Select the correct atomic symbol for an isotope of nitrogen with 8 neutrons.
questions.explanation: Nitrogen has 7 protons; with 8 neutrons the mass number is 7 + 8 = 15, written 15N (N-15). The leading superscript is the mass number, not the proton or neutron count alone.

hints.prompt: How is an isotope's mass number related to its protons and neutrons?
hints.rationale: Mass number is the total count of protons plus neutrons.

hints.prompt: Nitrogen has 7 protons and this isotope has 8 neutrons. Which quantity belongs in the superscript?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sum of the protons and the neutrons

choices.label: D
choices.text: The difference between neutrons and protons

hints.rationale: The superscript (mass number) is protons + neutrons for this isotope.

hints.prompt: Which two choices are traps that place the proton count or the neutron count in the superscript instead of the mass number?
hints.rationale: 7N and 8N wrongly use the proton or neutron count as the superscript rather than their sum.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q6 = d).

questions.stem: The average atomic mass of chlorine is 35.4527 amu. There are two stable isotopes of chlorine: Cl-35 and Cl-37. Given this information, which of the statements below is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The fractional abundance of Cl-35 is greater than that of Cl-37.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fractional abundance of Cl-37 is greater than that of Cl-35.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fractional abundances of Cl-35 and Cl-37 are equal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The relative fractional abundances of Cl-35 and Cl-37 cannot be determined from this information.

questions.explanation: A weighted-average atomic mass lies closest to the mass of the most abundant isotope. Because 35.45 is much nearer 35 than 37, Cl-35 must be the more abundant isotope.

hints.prompt: Where does an element's weighted-average atomic mass fall relative to its isotopes' masses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Closest to the mass of the most abundant isotope

choices.label: C
choices.text: Closest to the least abundant isotope

hints.rationale: A weighted average is pulled toward whichever isotope is more abundant.

hints.prompt: The average is 35.45, between 35 and 37. Which isotope's mass is it closer to?
hints.rationale: 35.45 is much nearer 35 than 37.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated using the average-mass reasoning?
hints.rationale: Equal abundance would give an average of 36, and the average clearly does determine the dominant isotope, so both are ruled out.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q7 = a).

questions.stem: How many valence electrons does an atom of phosphorus have?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus is in group 15 (VA) of the periodic table, so a neutral P atom has 5 valence electrons. (15 is its total electron count, not its valence count.)

hints.prompt: For a main-group element, how do you find the number of valence electrons?

choices.label: A
choices.text: From its main-group number (groups 1-2 and 13-18 give 1-8)

choices.label: C
choices.text: From its total number of electrons

hints.rationale: A main-group element's valence count matches its group number.

hints.prompt: Which number tied to phosphorus should be used as its valence count?
hints.rationale: Use the main-group number, not the total electron count, to get valence electrons.

hints.prompt: Which choice is the trap that reports phosphorus's TOTAL electrons instead of its valence electrons?
hints.rationale: 15 is phosphorus's total electron count (its atomic number), not its number of valence electrons.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q8 = b).

questions.stem: To achieve an octet in its valence shell, sulfur must …
questions.explanation: Sulfur is in group 16 with 6 valence electrons, so it must gain 2 electrons to reach a stable octet of 8, forming the sulfide ion S2-.

hints.prompt: According to the octet rule, how do main-group atoms achieve a stable valence shell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By gaining, losing, or sharing electrons to reach 8 valence electrons

choices.label: B
choices.text: By always losing all of their valence electrons

choices.label: D
choices.text: By adding protons to the nucleus

hints.rationale: Atoms react to attain a full octet of eight valence electrons.

hints.prompt: Sulfur (group 16) has 6 valence electrons. What is the most direct route to an octet?
hints.rationale: With 6 valence electrons, gaining electrons is the short path to 8.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because sulfur reaches an octet by gaining electrons, not losing them?
hints.rationale: A group-16 atom with 6 valence electrons gains electrons toward an octet, so both 'lose' options are wrong.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q9 = c).

questions.stem: Based on your knowledge of the periodic variation in atomic size, which of the following correctly orders the elements in terms of decreasing atomic radius?
questions.explanation: Atomic radius decreases from left to right across a period (increasing effective nuclear charge). For the period-2 elements B, N, and F, decreasing radius is therefore B > N > F.

hints.prompt: How does atomic radius change across a period and down a group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreases left-to-right across a period; increases down a group

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increases left-to-right; decreases down a group

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreases both across a period and down a group

hints.rationale: Radius shrinks across a period (rising nuclear charge) and grows down a group (added shells).

hints.prompt: For the group-1 set Li, K, Cs, what is the correct order of DECREASING atomic radius?
hints.rationale: Atomic radius increases down a group, so cesium (bottom) is largest: Cs > K > Li.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be ruled out because they are not written in correctly decreasing order for a group-1 (Li, K, Cs) set?
hints.rationale: Down group 1 size increases (Cs > K > Li), so a decreasing order would be Cs, K, Li - neither listed group-1 order.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q10 = a).

questions.stem: Which atom is likely to form a cation?
questions.explanation: Potassium is an alkali metal (group 1); metals readily lose electrons to form cations (K+). Oxygen and bromine are nonmetals that tend to form anions, and argon is a noble gas that is generally unreactive.

hints.prompt: Which kind of element most readily loses electrons to form cations?
hints.rationale: Metals have low ionization energies and lose electrons to form cations.

hints.prompt: Oxygen and bromine are nonmetals. Do they tend to form cations or anions?

choices.label: C
choices.text: They stay neutral in all cases

hints.rationale: Nonmetals like O and Br gain electrons to form anions, not cations.

hints.prompt: Which choice is the trap that will not form a cation because it is an unreactive noble gas?
hints.rationale: Argon is a noble gas with a full octet, so it does not form a cation.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q1 = d).

questions.stem: Which is not a physical property of metals?
questions.explanation: Metals are good conductors (not insulators) of electricity, and are also good heat conductors, malleable, and ductile. 'Good insulator of electricity' is therefore not a property of metals.

hints.prompt: Which set of physical properties is characteristic of metals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Good conductors of heat and electricity, malleable, and ductile

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unable to conduct heat at all

hints.rationale: Metals are malleable, ductile, and good conductors of both heat and electricity.

hints.prompt: Electrical conductivity in metals comes from their freely moving (delocalized) electrons. This makes a metal best described as which of the following?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A conductor only when a nonmetal is added

hints.rationale: Delocalized electrons let metals conduct electricity well.

hints.prompt: This is an EXCEPT question. Which two options are unquestionably real metallic properties and can therefore be eliminated as the exception?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Good conductor of heat and good insulator of electricity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Malleable and good insulator of electricity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ductile and good insulator of electricity

hints.rationale: Malleability and ductility are hallmark metallic properties, so neither is the exception.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q2 = b).

questions.stem: What is the charge on sulfate?
questions.explanation: Sulfate is the polyatomic ion SO4^2-, which carries a 2- charge.

hints.prompt: What is true of a common polyatomic ion's charge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has a fixed characteristic charge for that ion's formula

choices.label: D
choices.text: It equals the number of atoms it contains

hints.rationale: Each common polyatomic ion has a specific, memorized charge.

hints.prompt: Sulfate is an oxoanion (an ion of oxygen combined with another element). Oxoanions carry what kind of charge?
hints.rationale: Oxoanions such as sulfate are negatively charged.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be ruled out because sulfate is an anion (negatively charged)?
hints.rationale: As an anion, sulfate must be negative, so the +1 and 0 options are impossible.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q3 = d).

questions.stem: Which element is a nonmetal?
questions.explanation: Sulfur (S) is a nonmetal. Lithium and calcium are metals (groups 1 and 2), and zinc is a transition metal.

hints.prompt: Where are nonmetals generally located on the periodic table?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the upper-right region of the table

choices.label: B
choices.text: On the far left, in groups 1-2

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only in the bottom two rows

hints.rationale: Nonmetals cluster in the upper-right of the periodic table.

hints.prompt: Zinc (Zn) sits in the transition-metal block of the periodic table. Is it a metal or a nonmetal?
hints.rationale: Zinc is a transition metal.

hints.prompt: Which choice is a trap for students who forget that zinc is a metal?
hints.rationale: Zinc is a transition metal, not a nonmetal, though its behavior can mislead.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q4 = b).

questions.stem: What is the correct chemical formula for the ionic compound formed between calcium and oxygen?
questions.explanation: Calcium forms Ca2+ and oxygen forms O2-. The 2+ and 2- charges balance in a 1:1 ratio, giving the empirical formula CaO.

hints.prompt: How do you combine a metal cation and a nonmetal anion into a neutral ionic formula?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Choose subscripts so the total positive and negative charges cancel

choices.label: B
choices.text: Always use a 1:1 ratio regardless of the charges

choices.label: C
choices.text: Add the charges together to get the subscript

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use the atomic masses as the subscripts

hints.rationale: Ionic formulas use the smallest whole-number ratio that makes the compound neutral.

hints.prompt: To balance Ca2+ with O2-, what must be true of the subscripts on Ca and O?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They must make the total positive charge equal the total negative charge

choices.label: B
choices.text: They must add up to the mass number

choices.label: D
choices.text: They must both be as large as possible

hints.rationale: The subscripts are set so the positive and negative charges balance.

hints.prompt: Which choice is a trap because it is not written in the lowest whole-number ratio?
hints.rationale: Ca2O2 has a 1:1 ratio but is not reduced to lowest terms, so it is not the proper empirical formula.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q5 = a).

questions.stem: What is the correct name of AlP?
questions.explanation: Aluminum has a single fixed charge of 3+, so no Roman numeral is needed. The monatomic anion of phosphorus is phosphide (P3-), so AlP is aluminum phosphide. Phosphite and phosphate are oxoanions, not present here.

hints.prompt: How do you name a binary ionic compound of a fixed-charge metal and a nonmetal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metal name + nonmetal root + '-ide', with no Roman numeral for a fixed-charge metal

choices.label: B
choices.text: Always add a Roman numeral for the metal

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use Greek prefixes such as di- and tri-

hints.rationale: Fixed-charge metal plus nonmetal-ide, with no Roman numeral needed.

hints.prompt: Aluminum has a single fixed charge (3+). What does that tell you about using a Roman numeral in the name of AlP?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Roman numerals are required for all metals

hints.rationale: Because aluminum's charge is invariant, its name needs no Roman numeral.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they name oxygen-containing anions that are not present in AlP?
hints.rationale: Phosphite and phosphate are oxoanions that contain oxygen, but AlP has none.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q6 = a).

questions.stem: What is the correct name of P2O5?
questions.explanation: P2O5 is a binary molecular (nonmetal-nonmetal) compound named with Greek prefixes: di- for the 2 phosphorus atoms and penta- for the 5 oxygen atoms, giving diphosphorus pentoxide.

hints.prompt: How are binary molecular (nonmetal-nonmetal) compounds named?

choices.label: A
choices.text: With Greek prefixes (di-, tri-, penta-, ...) showing each atom count

choices.label: B
choices.text: With Roman numerals for the charge

choices.label: C
choices.text: As ionic '-ide' names with no prefixes

hints.rationale: Molecular compounds use Greek prefixes to indicate the number of each atom.

hints.prompt: P2O5 has 2 phosphorus atoms and 5 oxygen atoms. Which prefixes correspond to those counts?
hints.rationale: Two is di- and five is penta-.

hints.prompt: Which two names can be eliminated because they fail to show five oxygen atoms with a 'pent-' prefix?
hints.rationale: Both omit the 'pent-' prefix needed for five oxygens.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q7 = b).

questions.stem: What is the correct formula for nitrous acid?
questions.explanation: The '-ous' acid corresponds to the '-ite' oxoanion. Nitrite is NO2-, so nitrous acid is HNO2. HNO3 (from nitrate, NO3-) is nitric acid.

hints.prompt: How do the '-ous/-ic' acid names relate to their '-ite/-ate' oxoanions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '-ous' acid comes from the '-ite' anion; '-ic' acid comes from the '-ate' anion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both endings refer to the same anion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acid names have no relation to anion names

hints.rationale: '-ous' acids come from '-ite' anions and '-ic' acids from '-ate' anions.

hints.prompt: 'Nitrous' acid maps to which nitrogen oxoanion family?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The '-ite' anion, which has fewer oxygens

choices.label: B
choices.text: The '-ate' anion, which has more oxygens

choices.label: D
choices.text: A species with no oxygen at all

hints.rationale: The '-ous' acid derives from the lower-oxygen '-ite' anion.

hints.prompt: Which choice is the trap corresponding to NITRIC acid (the nitrate '-ate' form) rather than nitrous acid?
hints.rationale: HNO3 is nitric acid, from nitrate (NO3-), the '-ic' form, not the '-ous' acid asked for.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q8 = c).

questions.stem: What is the correct name of Cr2S3?
questions.explanation: Sulfide is S2-, so three sulfides total -6. The two chromium ions must therefore total +6, i.e., Cr3+. Because chromium is a variable-charge metal, the name uses a Roman numeral: chromium(III) sulfide.

hints.prompt: When must an ionic compound's name include a Roman numeral for the metal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When the metal is a variable-charge (transition) metal

hints.rationale: Variable-charge metals need a Roman numeral to specify the cation charge.

hints.prompt: In Cr2S3 the three sulfides (S2-) total -6. What total positive charge must the two chromium ions supply?

choices.label: A
choices.text: +6 total, shared over the two Cr

hints.rationale: Charge balance requires the two Cr to total +6.

hints.prompt: Which two names can be eliminated as improperly formed for a variable-charge metal ionic compound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'chromium sulfide' (missing Roman numeral) and 'dichromium trisulfide' (uses molecular prefixes)

hints.rationale: A variable-charge metal needs a Roman numeral, and ionic compounds do not take Greek prefixes.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q9 = d).

questions.stem: What is the correct formula for ammonium sulfate?
questions.explanation: Ammonium is NH4+ and sulfate is SO4^2-. Two ammonium ions are needed to balance one sulfate, and the polyatomic cation is enclosed in parentheses: (NH4)2SO4.

hints.prompt: How do you build a neutral formula from a polyatomic cation and a polyatomic anion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Balance the total charges, enclosing a repeated polyatomic ion in parentheses

choices.label: B
choices.text: Write them side by side with no subscripts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Add the charges together to make the subscript

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use Greek prefixes for each ion

hints.rationale: Combine so charges cancel, using parentheses when a polyatomic ion repeats.

hints.prompt: Ammonium is NH4+ and sulfate is SO4^2-. How many ammonium ions balance one sulfate?
hints.rationale: Two +1 ammonium ions balance one -2 sulfate.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they contain sulfite (SO3), not sulfate (SO4)?
hints.rationale: Sulfate is SO4^2-; the SO3 options are sulfite and do not match.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
